unit.name: Optimization setup: loss landscapes, random search, and gradients
unit.description: Frames optimization as finding the best W in a loss landscape and contrasts random search with gradient-based search.
unit.summary: After defining data loss plus regularization, the lecture asks how to find the best W; this is the optimization problem [ts=1474s]. The loss-landscape analogy treats vertical position as loss and horizontal position as model parameters; the optimizer is like a blindfolded person who can only feel local slope [ts=1488s]. Random search is shown as a deliberately bad first idea: trying 1,000 random W values gives about 15.5% CIFAR-10 accuracy, far below modern roughly 99.7% accuracy [ts=1567s]. The better strategy is to follow the slope: in one dimension this is the derivative, and in multiple dimensions the gradient is the vector of partial derivatives [ts=1654s]. The slope in a direction is the dot product of the direction with the gradient, and steepest descent is the negative gradient [ts=1716s]. Numerical gradients perturb one coordinate by a small h, recompute loss, subtract, and divide by h; this is easy but slow and approximate [ts=1729s]. Analytic gradients use calculus and the chain rule to compute dW exactly and quickly, but they can be implementation-error-prone, so numerical gradients are used for gradient checking [ts=1883s].

key_points.text: Optimization asks how to find the best W once scores, data loss, and regularization are defined.
A loss landscape maps model parameters to loss, and the optimizer only gets local slope information.
Random search over W achieved about 15.5% CIFAR-10 accuracy, which is much worse than modern state of the art.
In multiple dimensions, the gradient is the vector of partial derivatives.
The steepest descent direction is the negative gradient.
Numerical gradients use finite differences such as (new_loss - old_loss)/h, but are slow and approximate.
Use analytic gradients in practice, but compare them to numerical gradients for gradient checking.

kp_catalog.name: Optimization objectives and loss landscapes
kp_catalog.description: The prior-lecture setup of score functions, losses, regularization, gradients, and optimization algorithms that neural-network training builds on. Using classification losses to score parameter settings and optimization algorithms such as SGD or Adam to search for low-loss solutions.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because optimization objectives and loss landscapes clarifies the prior-lecture setup of score functions, losses, regularization, gradients, and optimization algorithms that neural-network training builds on, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices though roles split between gateway and supporting.

kp_catalog.name: Gradient checking
kp_catalog.description: Central-difference finite-difference estimates provide a slow but useful way to verify that a manual backward implementation is correct. Finite-difference derivative approximations, exact calculus-based gradients, and implementation checks comparing the two.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because gradient checking formalizes how central-difference finite-difference estimates provide a slow but useful way to verify that a manual backward implementation is correct, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context while roles split between gateway and supporting and importance labels split between critical and medium.
